data.title: Linkhome Shares Soar After IPO
data.description:
By Katherine Hamilton

Linkhome shares are climbing following the real-estate company's initial public offering.

The stock gained 72% to $9.71 Friday after making its Nasdaq debut at $4 a share on Thursday.

Linkhome offered 1.5 million shares for a total of $6 million, above the 1.25 million shares it had said it planned to offer in a preliminary filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

Shares were paused several times on Thursday as the price swung to as high as $22.33 at one point, and closed at $5.63.

Linkhome offers a real-estate AI model called HomeGPT to help consumers buy, sell, finance and renovate houses. It aggregates listings, data analytics and transaction services in one place to help customers search for properties, calculate financing and schedule showings.

The Irvine, Calif., company plans to use the proceeds from its IPO to intensify its marketing and advance its technology, including rolling out its cash-off product.

Write to Katherine Hamilton at katherine.hamilton@wsj.com

data.title: Health Care Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Health Care sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1009 ET - Centene CEO Sarah London says its Medicaid portfolio fell short of expectations in 2Q. London on an earnings call adds the Medicaid portfolio produced an "unanticipated and unacceptable health benefits ratio of 94.9%." The managed-care specialist attributed the underperformance to a step up in medical costs in three areas: behavioral health, home health and high-cost drugs. London says behavioral health was the most significant driver of the quarter-over-quarter increase with applied behavioral analysis as an accelerating pressure point across a number of its markets. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0936 ET — Centene says an analysis of data from an independent actuarial firm confirmed a significant shift in the marketplace risk pool in 2025, which it sees being primarily driven by three things. CEO Sarah London on a 2Q earnings call notes that a higher-than-expected percentage of healthy and/or low-utilizing members left the marketplace during open enrollment. Additionally, new signups to the market had higher morbidity, likely reflecting changes in the underlying member mix, London adds. Lastly, a step up in marketplace utilization more broadly, combined with more aggressive provider coding, is likely driving higher-in-the-year documented morbidity, the CEO concludes. The managed-care specialist now expects 2025 earnings to be pressured by $2.4 billion, up from an earlier estimate of $1.8 billion. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0929 ET — Centene now expects 2025 earnings to be pressured by $2.4 billion, up from an earlier estimate of $1.8 billion. The managed-care specialist in July withdrew its guidance for the year and disclosed that it has data showing that enrollees in the marketplaces overall appeared to be fewer, and less healthy, than expected. As a result, it expects to get a smaller payout from a federal program designed to even out risk across insurers. Shares rise 3.5% to $27.90 in premarket trading despite a loss in 2Q and declines in membership for certain health insurance lines. (denny.jacob@wsj.com; @pennedbyden)

0445 ET - China's pharmaceutical sector is likely to sustain its strong momentum, HSBC analysts say in a research note. The sector has outperformed the market year-to-date, with the Hang Seng Healthcare Index up more than 70%, driven by breakthroughs in business development such as 3S Bio's licensing deals with Pfizer, they note. A further sector re-rating likely after a robust 1H performance, they say. HSBC maintains buy ratings on leading players including Jiangsu Hengrui Pharmaceuticals, Sino Biopharmaceutical and Hansoh Pharmaceutical Group. (tracy.qu@wsj.com)

0414 ET - China's plan to rectify a price war in the pharma industry should bode well for the sector, DBS says in a note. In future selections of medical-products suppliers for public hospitals, the lowest bid might not win, China's National Healthcare Security Administration says. DBS thinks this move alleviates the largest risk to China's pharma sector--price cuts. Chinese pharma stocks could have further room to grow as the valuation isn't high from a historical perspective, it says. In view of better industry environment, the market could raise pharma companies' 2H sales and earnings forecasts, it adds. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

2352 ET - If tensions between Thailand and Cambodia escalate, Carabao Group could face the largest impact, followed by Bumrungrad Hospital and Mega Lifesciences, CGS International analyst Kasem Prunratanamala says in a research note. Cambodia ranked 11th among Thailand's export destinations in 2024, with key exports including beverages, auto and motorcycle parts, engines and agricultural machinery--together accounting for over 30% of total exports. While there have been no significant effects on Thai companies so far, an escalation could hurt Carabao's 2025 earnings, as 28% of its revenue comes from energy drink sales in Cambodia, the analyst says. Bumrungrad and Mega Lifesciences could also be affected, with an estimated 6% of their 2025 revenues tied to Cambodia. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Financial Services Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Financial Services. Exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1055 ET - Phillips 66 reports a strong growth trajectory for its midstream business, which activist investor Elliott Investment Management has said should either be sold or spun off. Elliott, which got two of its nominees named as directors in May after a battle with the board, has said the midstream unit could be worth more than $40 billion. The segment generated $972 million in adjusted Ebitda for 2Q, up $87 million from last quarter, following the acquisition of EPIC NGL, which Phillips renamed Coastal Bend. The opening of another gas-processing plant in the Permian basin also boosted results, CEO Mark Lashier says in an interview with CNBC. "It opens up frontiers for more organic growth in our midstream business," he says. "So we really like that business, it's delivering results." (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1027 ET - The two Phillips 66 directors that activist investor Elliott Investment Management got installed after a boardroom battle with the oil refiner are "well integrated" among the other members, CEO Mark Lashier says in an interview with CNBC. Describing the fight with Elliott as "a big contest," Lashier says the Elliott nominees, Sigmund Cornelius and Michael Heim, are aligned with everyone else on expanding shareholder value and are bringing diverse perspectives as the board continuously reexamines its strategy. "Those perspectives are on the table, they're in the discussion," he says. The CEO pushes back when Cornelius and Heim are referenced as "Elliott board members." "They're Phillips 66 board members now," he says. "Their fiduciary responsibility is to all of the shareholders." (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0834 ET - EFG International's shares have had a strong run over the past few years after a string of positive results and surprises, Vontobel says in a research note after the Swiss bank's half-year print. "The strongly increased valuation seems to already anticipate further accretive M&A deals and/or higher financial targets to be announced at the upcoming Investor Day on Nov. 25," analyst Andreas Venditti writes. The bank is on track to exceed its targets from its previous three-year plan, except in terms of its cost-to-income ratio given the increased investments in hiring after Credit Suisse's collapse. But EFG has executed well and is on the lookout for more acquisitions, so it could aim higher when it lays out its next plan, he says. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0820 ET - Deutsche Bank's current trading adequately reflects the improved outlook for the German bank, ODDO BHF says in a research note. The group reported second-quarter results ahead of expectations with cost-control initiatives on track, analysts Roland Pfaender and Steven Gould write. Management expects a significant tailwind from Germany's planned infrastructure investment and defense spending initiatives, which will create business opportunities for the bank. Meanwhile, steps toward setting up a European investment and savings union are also set to be a boon for its asset management and private bank activities. Yet, this optimism is already priced into the stock and there is more upside with other names in the sector, the analysts say. The brokerage lowers its rating to neutral from outperform but lifts the target price to 29 euros from 26 euros. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0614 ET - Another policy rate cut by the European Central Bank this year shouldn't dent the earnings of the continent's banks, Keefe, Bruyette & Woods say in a research note after the central bank held rates steady at 2%. The market pencils in another cut by the end of the year though this seems far from certain given the ECB's reassuring and balanced press conference, analysts write. "As banks increase their [net interest income] resilience, each 25 basis point parallel shift is worth just 1% of sector earnings," they say. KBW continues to see the upward sloping yield curve as an important offset for bank earnings. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0510 ET - NatWest is well placed to weather a potential credit downturn given the resilience of its customers, the bank's finance chief tells CNBC. The bank's economic assumptions haven't moved materially despite all the noise, Katie Murray says. Impairment charges, which reflect bad loans and is a key metric for the group to see how financially healthy people are, are at historically low levels, she says. Signs of an economic deterioration aren't showing up in its numbers. "It's not something we see in the day to day activity of how people are behaving, the lending that people are taking out, or the money that they're depositing, or the investments we're seeing into shares," Murray adds. Shares are up 1.6% and 27% year to date. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0505 ET - The new midterm targets outlined by Sabadell at its investor day show that it is buckling up for growth, JB Capital says in a research note. This strengthens the view that suitor BBVA has to increase its offer price to secure meaningful acceptance, analysts write. They calculate that the offer can be enhanced by up to 48%. The Spanish bank's growth ambitions--driven by corporate and consumer loans as mortgages should align with the market--seem reasonable and cost of risk remains under control, they say. The lender's aim to add two to three percentage points to its 8% market share in the coming years seems realistic and in line with the country's nominal GDP growth expectations, they add. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0439 ET - Deutsche Bank's shares had a sharp rise on what was an average set of results and limited evidence of revenue progression in its corporate and private bank, Citi says in a research note. Citi cuts its rating on the German bank to sell from neutral. While reaction to the quarterly print seems to have been driven by commentary provided on regulatory risk-weighted asset inflation risk, this wasn't much of a concern for Citi analysts. Their fears are focused on whether Deutsche Bank will be able to meet its revenue targets and the higher bar for capital returns, they say. "The bank has become a 'concept stock' but after [an around] 60% re-rating, we would be very cautious on allocating more capital here, until there is clear evidence of earnings support flowing through," they say. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0411 ET - Close Brothers' disposal of its Winterflood unit for 104 million pounds in cash provides it with a capital buffer for the car loan probe ruling, Shore Capital says in a research note. "We see this as a decent price for a business that in recent times has been operating around a break-even level due to a combination of cyclical and structural factors, albeit perhaps less good if looked through the lens of historical profitability," analyst Gary Greenwood writes. Any excess capital after the probe is expected to be reinvested in other parts of the business or returned to shareholders, he says. Shares are up 11% at 455 pence. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0410 ET - NatWest's quarterly print shows that the bank is in a sweet spot after the government returned it to private ownership, Interactive Investor says in a market comment. The U.K. lender is the investor favorite in the sector and its results justify that optimism, analyst Richard Hunter writes. "It remains to be seen whether this new-found freedom will enable a more aggressive acquisition policy," he adds. The bank's significant cash generation brings up the dilemma of whether to boost shareholder returns, invest heavily in the business or pursue an acquisition, he says. The group also raised its returns and income guidance for the year and announced a 750 million pound share buyback, which has been well-received by investors. Shares rise 2% to 513 pence. (elena.vardon@wsj.com)

0350 ET - Close Brothers Group shares jumped as much as 12% to their highest price since September after the U.K. bank sold its execution services and securities business Winterflood to Marex Group for around 104 million pounds. "This is a positive transaction for CBG, particularly given speculation as recently as April that the business may be sold for only circa 40 million pounds," Keefe, Bruyette & Woods analysts Edward Firth and Elise Yu Ge write in a research note. The deal also simplifies the group and removes a business that brought in considerable levels of uncertainty and volatility over its earnings outlook, they add.(elena.vardon@wsj.com)

2159 ET - Singapore banks are likely to weather continuing trade-induced volatility, UOB Kay Hian's Jonathan Koh says in a note. Higher U.S. reciprocal tariffs are set to kick in Aug. 1, which could drive up near-term inflation. The Federal Reserve is therefore likely to delay interest-rate cuts, which could benefit Singapore's lenders. Fed Chair Jerome Powell's term expires next May. A more dovish Fed chair could lead to higher inflation, too. Koh keeps his overweight rating on Singapore's banking sector, as lenders provide attractive dividend yields supported by capital management. The brokerage names OCBC as its top pick, maintaining its buy call and S$19.25 target price on OCBC given the bank's focus on Asean trade and investment flows. Shares of OCBC are last at S$17.21. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

data.title: Energy & Utilities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Energy and Utilities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1055 ET - Phillips 66 reports a strong growth trajectory for its midstream business, which activist investor Elliott Investment Management has said should either be sold or spun off. Elliott, which got two of its nominees named as directors in May after a battle with the board, has said the midstream unit could be worth more than $40 billion. The segment generated $972 million in adjusted Ebitda for 2Q, up $87 million from last quarter, following the acquisition of EPIC NGL, which Phillips renamed Coastal Bend. The opening of another gas-processing plant in the Permian basin also boosted results, CEO Mark Lashier says in an interview with CNBC. "It opens up frontiers for more organic growth in our midstream business," he says. "So we really like that business, it's delivering results." (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1036 ET - Phillips 66 CEO Mark Lashier says the California administration has been "very supportive" of its upcoming closure of a refinery near Los Angeles, which is slated to cease operations in 4Q. Driving that support is the company's plan to resupply the refined products that will be going away, the CEO says. Phillips has already done that in Northern California when it converted a facility there from crude oil to renewable feedstocks, so it has "a proven ability to bring in feedstocks from outside California," Lashier says. Asked whether the business environment in California had become too costly to operate in, the CEO responds that the LA facility was very old, had challenging inefficiencies, was its highest-cost refiner and wasn't contributing meaningfully to earnings. "We were at a point where we would have to recapitalize that asset and we just didn't see the path forward for profitability," he says. (dean.seal@wsj.com)

1027 ET - The two Phillips 66 directors that activist investor Elliott Investment Management got installed after a boardroom battle with the oil refiner are "well integrated" among the other members, CEO Mark Lashier says in an interview with CNBC. Describing the fight with Elliott as "a big contest," Lashier says the Elliott nominees, Sigmund Cornelius and Michael Heim, are aligned with everyone else on expanding shareholder value and are bringing diverse perspectives as the board continuously reexamines its strategy. "Those perspectives are on the table, they're in the discussion," he says. The CEO pushes back when Cornelius and Heim are referenced as "Elliott board members." "They're Phillips 66 board members now," he says. "Their fiduciary responsibility is to all of the shareholders." (dean.seal@wsj.com)

0957 ET - Oil futures are on track to end the week little changed, supported by the U.S. inching toward a trade deal with the EU and a second weekly draw in U.S. crude oil stocks, while additional OPEC+ output keeps a lid on advances. The negative reaction yesterday to the Trump administration's authorizing Chevron to resume production in Venezuela was brief. "The thought is that this could bring around 250,000 b/d of heavy crude supplies to the U.S.--as opposed to Asian buyers," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. "This distillate-rich crude oil could help alleviate some tightness in the fuel." WTI is off 0.1% at $65.93 a barrel, and Brent is flat at $69.18 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0549 ET - The market is nervous about the sustainability of TotalEnergies' unfunded shareholder distributions, HSBC analysts write. The concerns, coupled with a jump in net debt, caused shares to drop 3.5% on Thursday despite the French energy major reporting only a small miss to earnings expectations, they write. Reassurance around second-half cash flows didn't quell the concerns. A softer environment in its LNG and downstream division could threaten its cash flow guidance, they write. TotalEnergies' dividend growth has been generous in recent years but it must be reined in, the analysts say. They cut their forecast for annual dividend per share growth to between 3% to 3.5% from between 5% to 6%. Shares fall 0.4% at 51.25 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0443 ET - Eni delivered a good set of results, with net profit and earnings before interest and taxes beating expectations, Jefferies analysts Giacomo Romeo andKai Ye Loh write. This is mainly due to a strong performance in its gas and liquefied natural gas division and a lower general tax rate, they write. Investors will likely question Eni's management on the details of its joint venture with Petronas, sources of cost reductions and the timeline for final investment decisions on Friday's earnings call, they write. The call is scheduled for 12:00 GMT. Shares trade flat at 14.408 euros.(adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

0417 ET - Eni is set for a strong second half of the year and 2026 as its divestment plan and self-help measures deliver results, RBC Capital Markets' Biraj Borkhataria and Adnan Dhanani write. The Italian oil major's balance sheet is more resilient than it has been for many years and free cash flow is growing, they write. Eni reported a decent set of second-quarter results, with a strong performance in its global gas and LNG unit delivering a 5% beat to expectations for group earnings before interest and taxes, they add. Shares trade flat at 14.40 euros. (adam.whittaker@wsj.com)

1504 ET - Oil rises for the first time in five sessions with help from expectations of the U.S. reaching a trade deal with the EU, while continuing to trade in a tight range. "The price action indicates market participants are getting increasingly sanguine about the outlook for oil markets, despite the accelerated phase-out of additional voluntary adjustments by OPEC+," Amarpreet Singh of Barclays says in a note. Oil stocks in the U.S., refining cracks and high-frequency trade flows continue to signal robust demand, "while production in the U.S. seems to be rolling over," he adds. "Based on the weekly estimates from the EIA and corporate commentary, we think risks to our U.S. crude oil production forecasts are skewed lower." WTI settles up 1.2% at $66.03 a barrel, and Brent rises 1% to $69.18 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

1426 ET - Oil futures are gaining but off earlier highs after WSJ reports that the Trump administration plans to allow Chevron to again pump oil in Venezuela, citing people familiar with the plan. The U.S. sources heavy crude oil from Venezuela and other countries such as Canada and Mexico. The U.S. imported 170,000 barrels a day of Venezuelan oil in April before Chevron's license expired, according to EIA data. That was down from almost 300,000 b/d in December and January. WTI is up 0.5% at $65.58 a barrel, and Brent is up 0.3% at $68.72.(anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

data.title: Basic Materials Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Basic Materials. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

0231 ET - Market sentiment on lithium stocks has improved due to a potential fall in lithium supply, DBS analysts write in a note. Zangge Mining's production halt in its subsidy Golmud due to compliance issues, combined with expected declines in overseas lithium supply from Chile, Australia's Bald Hill mine, and Africa in 3Q, have fuelled expectations reduced lithium supply, they say. Also, Chinese government's recent campaign to curb excessive competition has improved market sentiment for industries facing over-capacity, including lithium, they say. The marginal supply reduction in 3Q could support a short-term rebound in lithium prices, they add. DBS maintains a buy rating on Tianqi Lithium shares and raises its target price to HK$50 from HK$35. They were recently at HK$40.35.(jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

2211 ET - Gold miner Newmont is target to meet FY guidance following stronger-than-expected 2Q production results, says Citi analyst Alexander Hacking in a note. "1H production from core mines was 2.8 million ounces, leaving the company well on track for [its] 5.6-million-ounce guidance," given output from Nevada Gold Mines is 2H weighted, says Hacking. He also highlights Newmont's record free cash flow, boosted by strong gold prices. Citi has a buy rating on Newmont, and expects the stock to outperform, says Hacking. Newmont is up 3.3% in Sydney at A$94.92. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2052 ET - Whitehaven's 4Q run-of-mine output beats consensus expectations and translates to strong sales, Jefferies analyst Daniel Roden says in a note. The miner's realized price is 5% lower than analysts anticipated, but offset by a strong result on costs, says Roden. Net debt is also better than expected. Roden says Whitehaven's balance sheet looks resilient at a time when coal prices have been weak. "Good operational control and an improving coal market outlook enhance expectations of deleveraging and capital returns," he says. Jefferies has a buy rating and A$6.40 target on Whitehaven. The stock is down 0.1% at A$7.02, outperforming Australia's materials index, which is sharply lower.(rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

2000 ET - Whitehaven recorded a good cost performance for FY25, says Citi analyst Paul McTaggart, after what he calls a "robust" 4Q result from the Australian coal miner. McTaggart says Whitehaven's 4Q saleable production was 9% higher than Citi expected. Cost guidance for FY26 will come next month with Whitehaven's FY fiscal results, he notes. Citi has a buy rating on the stock, with a target price of A$7.00. Whitehaven ended Thursday at A$7.03/share. (rhiannon.hoyle@wsj.com; @RhiannonHoyle)

1407 ET - Hopes that negotiations between the EU and US yield a trade deal are spurring optimism, resulting in decreased demand for gold. But investors are keeping a guarded view on the outcome. "Markets are remaining cautious over the trade picture with South Korea and India, which are both still negotiating deals and could face steep tariffs as well," says ADM Investor Services in a note. The firm also notes that strong demand from central banks around the world will also support elevated gold prices. The front-month contract finishes down 0.7% to $3,371 per troy ounce. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

data.title: Auto & Transport Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering the Auto and Transport sector. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires at 4:20 ET, 12:20 ET and 16:50 ET.

1022 GMT - Hesai Group is likely to benefit from the rapid expansion of China's robotaxi fleet, leveraging its solid position in the robotaxi LiDAR market, Daiwa analysts write in a note. Hesai may maintain robust LiDAR delivery momentum and reach breakeven in 2Q, they add. The company has long dominated the global robotaxi LiDAR market thanks to the performance and cost advantages of its mechanical LiDAR. Chinese robotaxi operators began large-scale commercialization of their services in 2025, cutting costs by adopting semi-solid-state LiDAR to replace more expensive mechanical versions, an area where Hesai again holds a competitive edge, they say. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

1017 GMT - The more-than $600 million tariff hit to Hyundai Motor's 2Q operating profit was double what HSBC expected, but analyst Will Cho thinks the tariff narrative has peaked. HSBC's sensitivity analysis suggests that a tariff reduction to 15% from 25% for South Korea could boost its 2025 operating profit forecast for the automaker by 13%. Mitigation efforts such as reducing incentives, increasing local production and diversifying supply chains will likely further blunt the tariff impact, Cho says. He also views the company's record revenue, which came despite a weak dollar, as encouraging. HSBC maintains a buy rating on the carmaker with a target price of KRW270,000, saying the stock offers compelling value following a sharp pullback. Shares last closed at KRW216,500. (farah.elias@wsj.com)

1014 GMT - Traton's downgrade to its 2025 guidance suggests double-digit cuts to consensus estimates for next year are inevitable, Bernstein analysts say in a research note. "[This year's] headwinds are well known and we would be happy--as many investors clearly have been given the strength of truck share prices--to look through this if 2026 expectations looked reasonable," the analysts say. However, the German truck and bus maker's new guidance implies it will need earnings to grow by a third in 2026 to meet consensus estimates, according to Bernstein. Shares fall 2.7%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0958 GMT - Traton cut its full-year guidance after a first-half performance that was in line with expectations, and its new outlook raises questions about pricing and order momentum, Bernstein analysts say in a research note. First-half orders were slightly lower than expected, the analysts say. While a reduction in the German bus and truck maker's guidance for full-year revenue and unit sales expectations was fully anticipated, its new profitability guidance is below consensus estimates, according to Bernstein. This implies a decline in Traton's adjusted operating margin for the second half similar to that of the first and double-digit downgrades to consensus estimates, Bernstein says. Shares fall 2.9%. (adria.calatayud@wsj.com)

0946 GMT - Pony AI is likely to break even on a unit level for its robotaxi fleet starting in 2026, Daiwa analysts write in a note. As the company's fleet grows, it may report positive operating profit and net profit from 2029, the analysts add. Pony AI could see revenue growth accelerate from 2027 when its robotaxi fleet is projected to reach 10,000 across China's four biggest cities. Pony AI is at the forefront of shaping the future of urban mobility, and has developed an innovative business model for ride-hailing and is actively engaging with regulators to enable the commercialization of autonomous driving, Daiwa says. It initiates coverage of the stock with a buy rating at a target price of $20.00. Shares last closed at $14.45. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0900 GMT - Volkswagen's new guidance is already under pressure due to a profit warning from subsidiary Traton, especially at the free cash flow level, UBS analyst Patrick Hummel writes. The bank expects slight negative consensus revisions, mainly on free cash flow. Volkswagen now sees flat revenue on year from up to 5% growth previously, with an operating profit margin of 4%-5%. Free cash flow is now guided at 1 billion-3 billion euros. "However, the new guidance doesn't include last night's profit warning from Traton, which shaves off 1 billion euros free cash flow and around 500 million euros of operating profit at VW group level." Shares rise 2.6%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0838 GMT - Grab could gain market share in the delivery space through its new budget-friendly products, says DBS's Sachin Mittal in a note. The group is expected to see higher order frequency, as such products include lower-cost delivery options and are likely to expand the company's Southeast Asia customer base. Its current monthly active users in the delivery and ride-hailing businesses cover only about 6% of the population in the region. DBS raises its FY 2025 and FY 2026 forecasts for adjusted Ebitda by 1% and 4%, respectively, and maintains a buy call on the stock. It raises its target price to $6.35 from $5.68. Shares last traded at US$5.34. (megan.cheah@wsj.com)

0741 GMT - Volkswagen earnings show that tariffs are biting as the company lowered its full-year guidance, Bernstein analysts write. Second-quarter results are mildly ahead of consensus, with VW noting an encouraging underlying performance at VW Passenger Cars excluding tariff impacts and restructuring charges. The operating result was 3.0% above Visible Alpha consensus of 3.7 billion euros, Bernstein adds. Audi and Porsche were the biggest underperformers for the quarter, with Audi's operating profit down 64% on year and Porsche's down 91%. The pair's operating profits missed Visible Alpha consensus by 48% and 61%, respectively, the bank says. Shares fall 3.3%. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0711 GMT - Volkswagen's core brands of VW, Skoda, Seat, Cupra and Volkswagen Commercial Vehicles showed resilience in the second quarter, while the group guidance cut was mostly as expected, Jefferies analysts write. The company posted a resilient second-quarter margin of 4.3% after a 1.2 billion-euro tariff impact in the quarter, they say. The core brands delivered a 6.3% margin from 3.8% last year, while there was the expected poor showings from Audi and Porsche. Audi is due to improve from the third quarter, but Porsche's guidance still looks at risk, the bank adds. As expected, VW reduced guidance to an operating profit margin of 4%-5% and compares with consensus at 4.7%. The working capital outflow is still a weak link, they add. (dominic.chopping@wsj.com)

0651 GMT - Ningbo Tuopu Group's revenue growth is likely to weaken in 2Q on lower-than-expected sales at key customers including Tesla, Seres and BYD, Jefferies analysts write in a note. The auto-parts supplier's management expects single-digit percentage revenue growth in 2Q, they say. The relatively low content value per vehicle for Geely limited contribution to Tuopu's revenue despite strong volume growth, Jefferies says. Ningbo Tuopu also supplies components for Tesla's robotaxi, with a content value per vehicle of around CNY2,000. The company says that its D&A expenses account for 5% to 7% of revenue, and it expects the figure to approach or exceed 7% when revenue growth slows, they add. Jefferies maintains its buy rating for the stock. (jiahui.huang@wsj.com; @ivy_jiahuihuang)

0629 GMT - SIA Engineering's earnings outlook seems positive, but appears reflected in the share price, CGS International's Raymond Yap says in a research report. Benefits from the company's new contracts with Singapore Airlines group are expected to become more evident in its earnings in future quarters. However, start-up costs and gestation losses for the company's new facilities are likely to increase in the coming quarters as progress is made toward full commercial operations. The brokerage maintains the stock's target price of S$3.10, but downgrades the rating to reduce from add as it reckons the share price adequately reflects the positive earnings trajectory. Shares are up 0.3% at S$3.31. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

0601 GMT - ComfortDelGro's "low-beta" stock status could change soon, Maybank Research's Eric Ong reckons. Its stock is supposedly one of the prime candidates to benefit from the MAS' deployment of its S$5 billion Equity Market Development Programme, the analyst says in a research report. Also, autonomous vehicles may be on the way soon. The multimodal transport operator launched its first two-year robotaxi pilot program in China's Guangzhou in partnership with Pony.ai, an autonomous mobility company, the analyst notes. The brokerage maintains the stock's buy rating, but raises the target price to S$1.70 from S$1.64 to reflect lower weighted average cost of capital. Shares are 5.8% higher at S$1.63. (ronnie.harui@wsj.com)

data.title: First Hawaiian Inc reports results for the quarter ended June 30 - Earnings Summary
data.description:

  • First Hawaiian Inc shares had risen by 1.0% this quarter and lost 2.9% so far this year.

data.title: Oil Retreats on Venezuelan Supply
data.description:
WTI crude futures fell over 1% toward $65 per barrel on Friday, erasing gains from previous session as the prospect of increased oil supply from Venezuela pressured prices.

The United States is preparing to allow partners of Venezuela’s state-run PDVSA, including Chevron, to operate with limitations, potentially boosting Venezuelan exports by over 200,000 barrels per day.

This added supply could ease tightness in the heavier crude market, which would benefit US refiners.

At the same time, oil prices found some support earlier in the week from disruptions to exports through the Black Sea and the Turkish port of Ceyhan, although flows have since resumed.

Hopes for new trade agreements between the US and partners like Japan and the EU also added some optimism to the market.

However, with the OPEC+ meeting approaching and no major policy shifts expected, the outlook for WTI remains steady but cautious.

data.title: Meta shares up modestly after co says to end EU political ads
data.description:
** Meta Platforms on Fri said it will end political, election, and social issue ads in EU in early Oct due to legal uncertainties from new EU rules; shares last up 0.3%

** The gain in META shares compared to a 0.2% rise in the Nasdaq

** Decision follows Google's similar move last Nov, highlighting Big Tech's resistance to EU's Transparency and Targeting of Political Advertising regulation aimed at increasing accountability

** The social media co reports qtrly results on Wed, and analysts on avg expect rev up 15% to $44.8 bln

** META's stock surged 23% YTD, outperforming Nasdaq , up 9% in same period

** Stock recently traded at 26x expected earnings, expensive vs its 5-year avg forward PE of 21

data.title: Margin Debt Hits New Record. Investor Sentiment Nears 'White Hot' Level, Warns Analyst. — Barrons.com
data.description:
By Andrew Welsch

Investors are trading on margin like it's 1999. Debt balances in investors' margin accounts topped $1 trillion in June, a new record, according to data from Finra, the brokerage industry's self-regulatory organization.

The figure surpasses the previous high of $937 billion in February and is a sign that investors' appetite for risk dramatically increased in May and June following a sharp selloff in U.S. stocks in April when President Donald Trump unveiled sweeping tariffs.

Margin trading can amplify returns and losses because investors borrow funds from their broker to buy shares. Investors' use of margin tends to parallel moves in stock markets, and the S&P 500 and Nasdaq have risen considerably since April.

Investors' use of margin has surpassed the level seen during the pandemic (before this year the record was in October 2021) when many first-time investors downloaded commission-free apps, such as Robinhood, to buy GameStop and other so-called meme stocks.

Risk signal. Use of margin signals increased investor appetite for risk and can be a sign that a bubble is building. Strategists at Deutsche Bank warn that we may be entering worrisome territory, noting that the 18% increase in margin usage is among the fastest increases since the tech bubble of the late 1990s. The fastest two-month increase in margin debt — 24.6% — was recorded in December 1999, according to Deutsche Bank. The second fastest — 20.3% — was notched in May 2007.

"We care about margin debt, as it is a useful gauge of when market sentiment is on the verge of going from 'red-hot' to 'white-hot'," credit strategists led by Steve Caprio write in a July 24 research note. "We have tracked this indicator for years now, but only have written on it today, given that the rate of increase is starting to flag as 'too hot" by our metrics. And while there is still room for market euphoria to potentially grow, we are ultimately getting closer to that point where market euphoria is becoming too hot to handle."

Caprio and colleagues, who focus on investment-grade and high-yield corporate bonds, say that the current run-rate of margin debt growth is consistent with U.S. high-yield credit spreads widening between 80 to 120 basis points over the next 12 months. A basis point is one one-hundredth of a percentage point and credit spreads widening is another way of saying high yield bonds fall in value relative to Treasuries. High-yield bonds falling in price can correlate with risk-off behavior among investors more broadly.

How long investor euphoria — and appetite for risk — lasts is hard to say, and Caprio acknowledges that unexpected market catalysts could emerge, such as tariff rate reductions or a dovish Fed. "But in all scenarios, we would not lose sight of the broader theme; namely that the level and pace of margin debt growth today suggests market sentiment is starting to run too hot."

Write to Andrew Welsch at andrew.welsch@barrons.com

data.title: Levi & Korsinsky, LLP Investigates Philadelphia Insurance Companies Data Breach
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESS Newswire / July 25, 2025 / Philadelphia Insurance Companies disclosed that it suffered a data breach that compromised the sensitive personal and protected health data of individuals. This data breach has led to concerns over the security of sensitive personal and protected health information entrusted to PHLY.

WHAT'S THIS ABOUT?

On June 9, 2025, Philadelphia Insurance Companies' information security team received an alert regarding suspicious activity on its network systems. Upon detection, PHLY disconnected affected systems to contain the threat and launched an investigation with the assistance of third-party forensic experts to determine the nature and scope of the incident. The investigation has determined that there was unauthorized access to PHLY's systems

These impacted files may have included names and other sensitive personal and private health information.

As of July 10, 2025, PHLY's investigation is ongoing, and its systems are still being restored. PHLY has not yet confirmed the extent of the unauthorized access or the data involved. Compensation may be available for those individuals who receive notice that their sensitive personal information was compromised.

WHY YOU MAY BE ENTITLED TO COMPENSATION?

Data breaches are serious matters that can cause long-term damage. Hackers may use stolen information to commit identity theft, financial fraud, or other crimes. Companies that fail to secure your personal data may be held liable for the resulting harm.

data.title: Global Commodities Roundup: Market Talk
data.description:
The latest Market Talks covering Commodities. Published exclusively on Dow Jones Newswires throughout the day.

1106 ET - Base metal prices fall, with LME three-month copper down 0.5% at $9,806.0 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum down 0.2% at $2,641.0 a ton. Base metals have slid on a stronger U.S. dollar, making it more expensive for international purchasers to buy dollar-denominated commodities and damping demand. The dollar has gained strength following a U.S.-Japan trade deal, raising market optimism for wider progress ahead of the Aug. 1 U.S. tariff reimplementation deadline. Still, the dollar remains depressed on month and in the year to date. Copper and aluminum remain up 0.8% and 2.9% on month, supported by the broadly weaker dollar and growing hopes for fresh Chinese demand after Beijing pledged new infrastructure investment. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1045 ET - Oil prices are trading within a narrow range. Uncertainty over the future of a U.S.-EU trade agreement persists after President Trump reportedly said there was a 50-50 chance that Washington would strike a deal with the bloc. Investors are now awaiting further announcements before Trump's Aug. 1 deadline. In afternoon trading, Brent crude is down 0.6% to $67.96 a barrel, while WTI falls 0.7% to $65.55 a barrel. The benchmarks are on track for weekly losses of 1.8% and 0.6%, respectively. "Oil prices have been trading in a narrow range since the beginning of July," Commerzbank Research's Barbara Lambrecht says. Looking forward, analysts continue to expect a significant oversupply as seasonal demand eases in the fall, which will likely put additional pressure on prices. (giulia.petroni@wsj.com)

1044 ET - Gold futures fall, and are on track to end the week 0.6% lower. Futures are down 1.1% at $3,336.50 a troy ounce. Concerns about a trade war between the U.S. and the European Union had allowed gold to break out of rangebound trading earlier in the week, but a U.S.-Japan trade agreement and optimism for more deals to come has brought the precious metal back into consolidation range, FxPro's Alex Kuptsikevich says in a note. Non-interest bearing gold is also vulnerable to declining hopes for a Federal Reserve rate cut, Kuptsikevich writes. If gold sharply falls below its 50-day moving average price in the new week, it will signal a transition from simply consolidating to a potentially severe correction, Kuptsikevich adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

1043 ET - The USDA announced a new round of flash sales of U.S. corn exports this morning, with Mexico purchasing 102,870 metric tons of corn for delivery in the 2025/2026 marketing year and South Korea buying 140,000 metric tons of corn for 2025/26. Mexico additionally purchased 142,500 metric tons of soybeans for 2025/26 delivery. While grain traders have wanted to see more confirmation of export demand, CBOT futures remain negative in morning trade. CBOT corn is down 0.4%, soybeans fall 0.6%, and wheat is down 1%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1032 ET - Live cattle futures on the CME are up 0.1%, with traders looking ahead to multiple USDA reports scheduled for release later today. In addition to the monthly Cattle on Feed and Cold Storage reports, the newest Cattle Inventory report is slated for release. This data will provide the latest assessment on the size of the U.S. cattle herd, which was reported in January to be at its smallest since the 1950s. The July report was not published last year, so this release will compare to data from two years ago. Allendale Inc. forecasts that the data will show a herd of roughly 93 million head, which would be down nearly 2 percentage points from 2023. Lean hog futures are 0.1% lower. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

1025 ET - The cost for a gallon of orange juice hasn't been this high since at least October 2001, according to data from Nielsen. Through mid-July, the average cost for a gallon was a record-breaking $11.68 a gallon. Retail prices are up 16.5% year-to-date, and almost 20% since this time last year. Orange juice futures on the ICE are lower in early trading, with the most-active contract down 4.3% to $3.2425 a pound. While weaker today, orange juice has surged 44% this month, with traders adding weather premium in what's expected to be a busy hurricane season. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0957 ET - Oil futures are on track to end the week little changed, supported by the U.S. inching toward a trade deal with the EU and a second weekly draw in U.S. crude oil stocks, while additional OPEC+ output keeps a lid on advances. The negative reaction yesterday to the Trump administration's authorizing Chevron to resume production in Venezuela was brief. "The thought is that this could bring around 250,000 b/d of heavy crude supplies to the U.S.--as opposed to Asian buyers," Alex Hodes of StoneX says in a note. "This distillate-rich crude oil could help alleviate some tightness in the fuel." WTI is off 0.1% at $65.93 a barrel, and Brent is flat at $69.18 a barrel. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0941 ET - Traders are focusing on weather in U.S. growing areas. A cold front is moving through the middle of the country, says the USDA in its daily weather report--separating milder temperatures in northern growing regions and hotter temperatures in southern portions. The cold front will move through the Corn Belt before dissipating, says the USDA. With crops maturing, traders find themselves taking a "wait-and-see" approach, says Naomi Blohm of Total Farm Marketing in a note. Most-active corn on the CBOT is down 0.5%, soybeans are off 0.7%, and wheat falls 0.7%. (kirk.maltais@wsj.com)

0929 ET - U.S. natural gas futures are fluctuating between small gains and losses while remaining sharply lower on the week as participants look beyond the current heat and high demand to a cooler outlook for August. "The next six days remain impressively hot and could end up being the hottest stretch of the summer," NatGasWeather.com says in a note. This week's selling reflects in part lower LNG feedgas flows and "a not hot enough weather pattern the first week of August." Nymex natural gas is up 0.2% at $3.101/mmBtu. (anthony.harrup@wsj.com)

0747 ET - Base metal prices are mixed, with LME three-month copper down 0.3% at $9,827 a metric ton and LME three-month aluminum up 0.15% at $2,650 a ton. That said, copper and aluminum are up 1.2% and 3.3% on month. Sentiment on the metals market is good with the London Metal Exchange index approaching highs last seen in March, Commerzbank analysts say in a note. Investors are likely to focus their attention more on upcoming sentiment indicators from China, rather than economic indicators from the U.S. or the eurozone, Commerzbank says. If Chinese indicators brighten, expectations for industrial-metals demand will increase and the metals index could push closer to previous highs, analysts say. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0744 ET - Gold futures slip as risk-on market sentiment prevails and the dollar strengthens. Futures are down 0.9% at $3,342.40 a troy ounce. The precious metal is under pressure on fading safe-haven demand after the U.S.-Japan trade deal, Tradu.com's Nikos Tzabouras says in a note. The deal is expected to reduce the hit from President Trump's tariff policies on both the global and U.S. economies, Tzabouras writes. This optimism, coupled with strong U.S. jobs data, has lowered hopes for imminent Federal Reserve interest-rate cuts. While this is positive for the dollar, it has weighed on noninterest bearing bullion, Tzabouras says. That said, gold's safe-haven appeal could quickly return on trade tensions ahead of the Aug. 1 tariff-reimplementation deadline and central bank demand remains supportive, he adds. (joseph.hoppe@wsj.com)

0625 ET - Palm oil ended lower amid weak exports. Malaysia's palm oil exports during the July 1-25 period are estimated to have fallen 15% on month at 896,484 metric tons, cargo surveyor AmSpec Agri Malaysia said Friday. Profit-taking activities after weeks of gains likely also weighed on prices. However, seasonal buying interest from India ahead of upcoming festivals provided support for the tropical oil's prices, analysts said. The Bursa Malaysia Derivatives October contract ended 54 ringgit lower at 4,276 ringgit a ton. (sherry.qin@wsj.com)

data.title: Avantic Medical Lab Data Breach - Levi & Korsinsky, LLP Launches Investigation
data.description:
NEW YORK CITY, NY / ACCESS Newswire / July 25, 2025 / Avantic Medical Lab disclosed that it suffered a data breach that compromised the sensitive personal and protected health data of individuals. This data breach has led to concerns over the security of sensitive personal and protected health information entrusted to Avantic Medical Lab.

WHAT'S THIS ABOUT?

On July 3, 2025, a ransomware group, Everest, leaked approximately 31 GB of Avantic Medical Lab's patient files on the dark web. The leaked files appear to include patient data relating to blood draws, insurance responses, and other correspondence. Avantic Medical Lab, a full-service clinical laboratory based in Edison, New Jersey, services hospitals and patients throughout New Jersey and New York.

These impacted files allegedly included sensitive personal and private health information, including patients' full names, addresses, telephone numbers, dates of birth, Social Security Numbers, medical records, health insurance information, diagnostic test results, and payment information.

At this time, Avantic Medical Lab has not yet issued an official notice to patients regarding the data breach. Compensation may be available for those individuals who receive notice that their sensitive personal information was compromised.

WHY YOU MAY BE ENTITLED TO COMPENSATION?

Data breaches are serious matters that can cause long-term damage. Hackers may use stolen information to commit identity theft, financial fraud, or other crimes. Companies that fail to secure your personal data may be held liable for the resulting harm.

data.title: Zephyr Closes Private Placement
data.description:
Halifax, Nova Scotia--(Newsfile Corp. - July 25, 2025) - Zephyr Minerals Ltd. (TSXV: ZFR) ("Zephyr" or the "Company") is pleased to announce, further to its news release of June 11, 2025, the Company has closed a non-brokered private placement through the issuance of 6,500,000 units (each a "Unit") for gross proceeds of $260,000 (the "Private Placement"). Each Unit consists of one common share (each a "Share") and one common share purchase warrant (each a "Warrant") of the Company. Each Warrant entitles the holder thereof to acquire one additional Share at a price of $0.08 for a period of thirty-six months from the date of issuance.

Net proceeds from the Private Placement will be used for general working capital purposes and to undertake a water monitoring well drill program on the Dawson Gold project. This program is in support of activities necessary to re-submit an application for a mining permit previously submitted in 2021.

In connection with the Private Placement, Zephyr paid finders' fees of $1,750 in cash and issued 43,750 Finders' Warrants to an arm's length party to the Company. Each Finders' Warrant entitles the holder to acquire one Share on the same terms as noted above. All securities issued in connection with the Private Placement are subject to a statutory hold period of four months and one day. The Private Placement remains subject to customary closing conditions, including approval from the TSX Venture Exchange.

Insiders of the Company acquired an aggregate of 3,850,000 Units in the Private Placement for proceeds of $154,000. Participation by insiders in the Private Placement constitutes a "related" party transaction" as defined under Multilateral Instrument 61-101 Protection of Minority Security Holders in Special Transactions ("MI 61-101"). The Company relied on the exemptions available under the instrument, and such participation was exempt from the formal valuation and minority shareholder approval requirements of MI 61-101 found in sections 5.5(a) and 5.7(1)(a) of MI 61-101 as the fair market value of insider subscriptions does not constitute more than 25% of the Company's market capitalization. The Company did not file a material change report more than 21 days before the expected closing date of the Private Placement as the details of the Private Placement and the participation therein by the insiders were not settled until shortly prior to the closing of the Private Placement, and the Company wished to close the Private Placement on an expedited basis for sound business reasons.

About Zephyr Minerals Ltd.

Zephyr Minerals is mission focused on obtaining a mining permit for its 100% owned Dawson Gold project in Colorado with the view to continuing to advance this project to the next stage of development. The Company continues to review gold properties for potential acquisition and/or joint venture throughout Zimbabwe as these are received from third parties. The Company continues to wait for the Zimbabwean Government to grant two Exclusive Prospecting Orders ("EPO"), covering 124,000 hectares applied for in 2021. The areas covered by the EPO applications are prospective for gold and lithium.
